did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Transfer Learning with the VGG16 architecture to classify seven types of skin diseases (including melanoma) using the ISIC 2018 dataset. The study explicitly compares the performance of a custom CNN against a transfer learning model to achieve classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Transfer Learning with the VGG16 architecture on the ISIC 2018 dataset to classify skin lesions. It includes a proposed methodology, experimental results with specific accuracy metrics (91.07%), and comparisons with existing works, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Convolutional Neural Network Based Skin Lesion Analysis for Classifying Melanoma
journal: 2019 International Conference on Sustainable Technologies for Industry 4.0 (STI)
brief_summary: This study proposes a machine learning approach using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Transfer Learning with the VGG16 architecture to classify seven types of skin diseases, including melanoma, from the ISIC 2018 dataset. The researchers compared a custom CNN model against a transfer learning model where VGG16 served as a feature extractor with frozen convolutional layers. The dataset consisted of 10,015 images, with 1,137 used for training and 197 for testing. Evaluation metrics included recall, sensitivity, and precision across the seven disease classes. The proposed transfer learning method achieved an accuracy of 91.07%, representing an 11.65% improvement over the custom CNN model's accuracy of 79.42%. The results indicate that transfer learning with VGG16 provides superior performance for automated skin lesion classification compared to the standard CNN architecture.

all_task_types.target: Seven types of skin diseases (Actinic Keratoses, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'classifying seven types of skin diseases' using CNN and Transfer Learning (VGG16) on the ISIC 2018 dataset. It reports accuracy, recall, and precision for these discrete diagnostic categories. The title also refers to 'Classifying Melanoma', indicating a classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018: Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 challenge containing dermoscopy images of skin lesions. The dataset includes images varying in source, location, size, and features of skin lesions.
classes_used: Actinic Keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/ Bowen’s disease (akiec)
benign keratoses like lesions (solar lentigines/seborrheic keratoses and lichen-planus like keratoses) (bkl)
vascular lesions (angiomas, angiokeratomas, pyogenic granulomas and hemorrhage) (vasc)

use.training: 1137 images were used for training the CNN and Transfer Learning (VGG16) models.
use.validation: The paper mentions calculating loss on validation data but does not specify a separate validation set count or images; it states '1137 images are used for training and another 197 images are used for testing'.
use.testing: 197 images were used for testing/classification to calculate accuracy, recall, and precision.
use.other: The remaining images from the total dataset (10015 - 1137 - 197 = 8681) were not used in the experimental evaluation described in this paper.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, recall, sensitivity, and precision metrics, with comparisons made based on absolute percentage improvements rather than statistical significance.

CNN (Proposed).notes: Baseline model using custom CNN architecture with 4 convolution layers, 2 Max pooling layers, and 2 Fully Connected layers. Input image size 400x648.

Transfer Learning using VGG16 (Proposed).notes: VGG16 used as feature extractor with frozen convolutional layers. Input images scaled to 224x224. Trained for 40 epochs.

ISIC 2018.notes: The Transfer Learning model achieved an 11.65% improvement in accuracy over the CNN model. The paper notes that loss values were calculated on training and validation data but does not report specific loss values in the text or tables, only referencing Figure 5.

architecture_used.main_model: Two models were used: a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Transfer Learning using VGG16.
architecture_used.pretraining: VGG16 was pre-trained on ImageNet (implied by standard VGG16 usage and reference [8] to Krizhevsky et al.). For the transfer learning model, the convolutional layers of VGG16 were frozen (pre-trained features kept constant).

custom_cnn.total_layers_summary: 4 convolution layers, 2 Max pooling layers, 2 Fully Connected layers

classification_or_output_head.custom_cnn: Fully Connected layer linked with SoftMax (implied by context of classification into 7 classes)
classification_or_output_head.transfer_learning_vgg16: Retrained Fully Connected layer with SoftMax activation

approximate_parameters.transfer_learning_vgg16: Only the Fully Connected layers were retrained; original VGG16 had 447,907,669 parameters but convolutional layers were frozen.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Images were resized to 400x648 pixels for the custom CNN input; images were scaled down to 224x224 pixels for the VGG16 transfer learning model.
Noise Removal: Median filtering was applied to remove noise and enhance image quality.
Contrast Enhancement: Image contrast was improved to better identify lesion areas.
Thresholding: Otsu thresholding technique was used to convert gray level images to binary images for feature extraction.
conclusions: Transfer learning using the VGG16 architecture as a feature extractor with frozen convolutional layers achieved a classification accuracy of 91.07% on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which is superior to the custom CNN model's accuracy of 79.42%.
The use of transfer learning resulted in an 11.65% improvement in accuracy compared to the standard Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture.
The proposed transfer learning method outperformed existing works, including those using VGG16 classifiers (80.1% and 79%), GoogLeNet (79.7%), and ResNet classifiers (82%).
The study confirms that CNN architecture is simple to apply for image classification, but transfer learning provides better performance for skin lesion analysis.
The authors acknowledge that while the current method is effective, future work will involve developing the transfer learning model with fine-tuning to further improve results.
important_remarks: The study uses a severely imbalanced dataset split, utilizing only 1,137 images for training and 197 for testing out of the 10,015 available in the ISIC 2018 dataset, which raises concerns about generalizability and statistical power.
The paper does not report specific loss values or detailed validation metrics in the text or tables, only referencing Figure 5, limiting the ability to assess convergence or overfitting.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance improvements are reported as absolute percentage differences (11.65%) without significance testing.

The custom CNN architecture is relatively simple (4 convolutional layers, 2 max pooling layers, 2 fully connected layers) compared to modern deep learning standards, potentially underrepresenting the baseline performance.

Transfer learning was implemented by freezing all convolutional layers of VGG16 and retraining only the fully connected layers, which limits the model's ability to adapt features specifically to the skin lesion domain compared to fine-tuning.

The paper acknowledges that future work will involve fine-tuning the transfer learning model, indicating that the current results may not represent the optimal performance achievable with the proposed methodology.

The dataset includes diverse skin lesion types with varying sources, locations, and sizes, but the small test set (197 images) may not adequately capture this heterogeneity, potentially biasing the reported accuracy of 91.07%.
